JavaScript Core Concepts (21) - Objects - How Objects Are Structured
Next up, we're starting the chapters on objects.
Defining an object is honestly really simple. You just write this:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
That way of defining an object is called an object literal (Object Literals). With an object literal, you can clearly see the property and the value of the object:
So an object is basically a property/value pairing, and the value can be anything — a primitive, another object, an array, even a function.
The other way to define an object is with a constructor:

Creating objects with a constructor isn't really recommended, and it comes with a few small gotchas. If you go the constructor route, it's not just that it reads worse than an object literal — try changing it to this:

var obj1 = new Object('1'); // String
var obj2 = new Object(1); // Number
You'll notice the object's type turns into something else entirely. We'll get into the details on that later.

Time to start the chapters on objects. We'll look at object literals, what a property/value pair actually is, and the other way to create objects using a constructor — plus why that constructor approach has a couple of gotchas you probably want to avoid.
